records.concept: Depression symptom — frequency of trouble keeping mind on what one was doing in the past week (CES-D item)

cde.id: Center for Epidemiologic Studies-Depression Scale (CES-D) - trouble keeping mind indicator

records.idealCde: Depression symptom — difficulty concentrating: frequency of trouble keeping one's mind on what one was doing during the past week. Question: 'How often did you have trouble keeping your mind on what you were doing?' Ordinal response scale: 1 = All of the time (5–7 days), 2 = Occasionally (3–4 days), 3 = Some of the time (1–2 days), 4 = Rarely or never (less than 1 day).

transforms.targetCdeId: Center for Epidemiologic Studies-Depression Scale (CES-D) - trouble keeping mind indicator
transforms.rationale: Source uses reverse ordering (1=most frequent to 4=least frequent) compared to CDE (0=least to 3=most frequent). All four frequency categories align perfectly in meaning: source 1 (All of the time 5-7 days) -> CDE 3, source 2 (Occasionally 3-4 days) -> CDE 2, source 3 (Some of the time 1-2 days) -> CDE 1, source 4 (Rarely or never <1 day) -> CDE 0.

transforms.targetCdeId: Are you deaf, or do you have serious difficulty hearing?

records.concept: Physician-diagnosed stroke or CVA (cerebrovascular accident) — ever told

cde.id: Have you ever been told by a physician that you had a stroke [PhenX]

records.idealCde: Physician-diagnosed cerebrovascular event (stroke/CVA or TIA/mini-stroke): whether a doctor has ever told the respondent that they have experienced a stroke or cerebrovascular accident (CVA) and/or a mini-stroke or transient ischemic attack (TIA). The ideal CDE asks: 'Has a doctor ever told you that you have had a stroke, CVA (cerebrovascular accident), mini-stroke, or TIA (transient ischemic attack)?' Answer type: categorical, dichotomous (Yes / No), self-reported, lifetime prevalence (ever diagnosed). Note: source fields capture these conditions both combined and separately (stroke/CVA as one item; TIA/mini-stroke as another; and a composite item covering stroke/CVA/TIA effects together); the ideal CDE set should include at minimum two parallel items — one for stroke/CVA and one for TIA/mini-stroke — each with response options 1=Yes, 2=No.

transforms.targetCdeId: Have you ever been told by a physician that you had a stroke [PhenX]
transforms.rationale: Direct 1:1 mapping between source codes and CDE values. Source code 1=Yes maps to CDE 'Yes', source code 2=No maps to CDE 'No'.

records.concept: Annual household income from all sources (categorical income-range brackets)

cde.id: Annual Household Income Range

records.idealCde: Social Determinants of Health (SDOH) — Neighborhood and Built Environment Assessment: A multi-item instrument capturing self-reported perceptions of the residential neighborhood's built environment and socioeconomic context. Covers: (1) Annual household income from all sources (self plus all household members, prior calendar year), measured as an ordinal categorical variable with income-range brackets (e.g., <$10,000; $10,000–$24,999; etc.); (2) Perceived access to retail/shopping within easy walking distance of home, rated on a 4-point Likert agreement scale (Strongly disagree / Somewhat disagree / Somewhat agree / Strongly agree) with a Don't know/Not sure option; (3) Perceived proximity of a public transit stop (bus, train, trolley, or tram) within a 10–15 minute walk from home, rated on the same 4-point Likert scale with Don't know/Not sure; (4) Perceived presence of sidewalks on most neighborhood streets, rated on the same 4-point Likert scale with additional 'Does not apply to my neighborhood' and Don't know/Not sure options; (5) Perceived availability of free or low-cost recreational facilities (parks, walking trails, bike paths, recreation centers, playgrounds, public pools, etc.) in the neighborhood, rated on the same 4-point Likert scale with Don't know/Not sure. All neighborhood perception items are anchored to the respondent's own residential neighborhood and home location. Answer types: ordinal categorical (income brackets) and ordinal Likert agreement scale (neighborhood items).

transforms.targetCdeId: Annual Household Income Range
transforms.rationale: All nine income bracket source codes map directly to their CDE equivalents by label (minor whitespace differences are inconsequential). PMI_PreferNotToAnswer ('Prefer not to answer') has no corresponding value in the CDE value set and is therefore unmapped.

records.concept: Perceived adequacy of household income and investments to maintain standard of living at retirement
records.idealCde: Social Determinants of Health (SDOH) — Neighborhood and Built Environment Assessment: A multi-item instrument capturing self-reported perceptions of the residential neighborhood's built environment and socioeconomic context. Covers: (1) Annual household income from all sources (self plus all household members, prior calendar year), measured as an ordinal categorical variable with income-range brackets (e.g., <$10,000; $10,000–$24,999; etc.); (2) Perceived access to retail/shopping within easy walking distance of home, rated on a 4-point Likert agreement scale (Strongly disagree / Somewhat disagree / Somewhat agree / Strongly agree) with a Don't know/Not sure option; (3) Perceived proximity of a public transit stop (bus, train, trolley, or tram) within a 10–15 minute walk from home, rated on the same 4-point Likert scale with Don't know/Not sure; (4) Perceived presence of sidewalks on most neighborhood streets, rated on the same 4-point Likert scale with additional 'Does not apply to my neighborhood' and Don't know/Not sure options; (5) Perceived availability of free or low-cost recreational facilities (parks, walking trails, bike paths, recreation centers, playgrounds, public pools, etc.) in the neighborhood, rated on the same 4-point Likert scale with Don't know/Not sure. All neighborhood perception items are anchored to the respondent's own residential neighborhood and home location. Answer types: ordinal categorical (income brackets) and ordinal Likert agreement scale (neighborhood items).

records.concept: Sitting height (rump-to-crown trunk length), continuous, cm

cde.id: Body height --sitting

records.idealCde: Sitting height (trunk length): the vertical distance from the sitting surface (rump) to the top of the head (crown), measured with the participant seated upright. Answers the question: 'What is the participant's sitting height (rump-to-crown distance)?' Expected answer type: continuous numeric value; units: centimeters (cm). Note: derived as the difference between total seated height (floor or reference surface to crown) and the height of the seating box used during measurement; qualifier is the seated/sitting position of the subject (self/participant).

records.concept: Reason standing height measurement was not performed

cde.id: Measurement not reported reason

records.idealCde: Sitting height (trunk length): the vertical distance from the sitting surface (rump) to the top of the head (crown), measured with the participant seated upright. Answers the question: 'What is the participant's sitting height (rump-to-crown distance)?' Expected answer type: continuous numeric value; units: centimeters (cm). Note: derived as the difference between total seated height (floor or reference surface to crown) and the height of the seating box used during measurement; qualifier is the seated/sitting position of the subject (self/participant).

transforms.targetCdeId: Measurement not reported reason
transforms.rationale: The CDE offers only two values: 'Not applicable' and 'Unknown'. 'Reason not known' (300) and 'Reason not known' (506) map to 'Unknown' as they explicitly indicate the reason is not known. All other source codes represent specific, known reasons (participant refusal, equipment issues, health conditions, nurse advice, etc.) that do not align with either 'Not applicable' or 'Unknown' — they are distinct, identifiable reasons with no matching CDE code. 'Not applicable' would imply the measurement was not attempted for a reason outside the scope of the question, but none of the source codes clearly convey that meaning. Confidence is moderate because the CDE is very coarse relative to the rich source taxonomy.

records.concept: Perceived neighborhood-level alcohol use (community norm/problem perception)
records.idealCde: Alcohol use behavior and environment — a cluster of related but distinct constructs measuring: (1) perceived neighborhood-level alcohol use (community norm/problem perception; question: 'Is there too much alcohol use in your neighborhood?'; answer: 5-point Likert agreement scale from Strongly Disagree to Strongly Agree); (2) self-reported frequency of any alcohol consumption in the past month (question: 'How often do you have a drink containing alcohol?'; answer: ordinal frequency categories: Never / Monthly or less / 2–4 times a month / 2–3 times a week / 4 or more times a week); (3) self-reported typical quantity of specific alcoholic beverage types (e.g., beer) consumed on weekdays in the past 12 months (question: 'In a typical week during the past 12 months, how many drinks of [beverage type] do you drink on weekdays?'; answer: numeric count of standard drinks, decimal); (4) self-reported frequency of heavy episodic drinking (4+ drinks per occasion) in the past 12 months, asked of females (question: 'About how often during the past 12 months did you have four or more drinks at the same sitting or occasion?'; answer: ordinal frequency categories from Almost every day to Never); and (5) self-reported overall frequency of any alcohol consumption in the past 12 months (question: 'About how often during the past 12 months did you drink alcohol?'; answer: ordinal frequency categories from Almost every day to Never). Member fields carry different qualifiers — neighborhood perception vs. self past-month vs. self past-12-months weekday quantity by beverage vs. self past-12-months heavy episodic drinking frequency [female-specific] vs. self past-12-months overall drinking frequency — and should not be collapsed into a single CDE.

transforms.rationale: All five source codes map exactly to the CDE value set; the labels are identical word-for-word.

transforms.targetCdeId: How often do you have a drink containing alcohol
transforms.rationale: Most mappings are straightforward. '01' (almost every day/6x week) and '02' (4-5x week) both map to '4 or more times a week'. '04' (once a week) maps to '2-4 times a month' as weekly frequency falls within that range (4 times/month). '06' (about once a month) and '07' (less than once a month) both map to 'Monthly or less'. Slight confidence reduction due to '04' once a week being borderline between '2-4 times a month' and '2-3 times a week', and '07' less than once a month being a slightly imprecise fit for 'Monthly or less'.

records.rationale: No candidate captures the specific combination of beverage-type breakdown (beer, red wine, white wine, liquor, other) by weekday vs. weekend day within the past 12 months; the closest candidates ask about average drinks on a typical drinking day without distinguishing beverage type or weekday/weekend granularity.

records.concept: Frequency of heavy episodic (binge) drinking (4+ drinks for females / 5+ drinks for males at one sitting, past 12 months)

cde.id: Five or more drinks in a period of 2 hours or less in past 12 months

records.idealCde: Alcohol use behavior and environment — a cluster of related but distinct constructs measuring: (1) perceived neighborhood-level alcohol use (community norm/problem perception; question: 'Is there too much alcohol use in your neighborhood?'; answer: 5-point Likert agreement scale from Strongly Disagree to Strongly Agree); (2) self-reported frequency of any alcohol consumption in the past month (question: 'How often do you have a drink containing alcohol?'; answer: ordinal frequency categories: Never / Monthly or less / 2–4 times a month / 2–3 times a week / 4 or more times a week); (3) self-reported typical quantity of specific alcoholic beverage types (e.g., beer) consumed on weekdays in the past 12 months (question: 'In a typical week during the past 12 months, how many drinks of [beverage type] do you drink on weekdays?'; answer: numeric count of standard drinks, decimal); (4) self-reported frequency of heavy episodic drinking (4+ drinks per occasion) in the past 12 months, asked of females (question: 'About how often during the past 12 months did you have four or more drinks at the same sitting or occasion?'; answer: ordinal frequency categories from Almost every day to Never); and (5) self-reported overall frequency of any alcohol consumption in the past 12 months (question: 'About how often during the past 12 months did you drink alcohol?'; answer: ordinal frequency categories from Almost every day to Never). Member fields carry different qualifiers — neighborhood perception vs. self past-month vs. self past-12-months weekday quantity by beverage vs. self past-12-months heavy episodic drinking frequency [female-specific] vs. self past-12-months overall drinking frequency — and should not be collapsed into a single CDE.

transforms.targetCdeId: Five or more drinks in a period of 2 hours or less in past 12 months
transforms.rationale: Most codes map reasonably well. Code 01 maps to 'Nearly every day' rather than 'Every day' since it explicitly includes 6x/week. Code 02 (4-5x/week) maps imperfectly to '3 to 4 times a week' as no better CDE option exists. Code 03 (2-3x/week) maps imperfectly to '2 times a week'. Code 07 ('less than once a month') is unmapped because it spans multiple sub-annual CDE categories (7-11, 3-6, or 1-2 times/year) with no way to determine the correct target.

transforms.targetCdeId: Five or more drinks in a period of 2 hours or less in past 12 months
transforms.rationale: Most codes map reasonably well. Code 01 maps to 'Nearly every day' rather than 'Every day' since it explicitly includes 6x/week. Code 02 (4-5x/week) maps imperfectly to '3 to 4 times a week' as no better CDE option exists. Code 03 (2-3x/week) maps imperfectly to '2 times a week'. Code 07 ('less than once a month') is unmapped because it spans multiple sub-annual CDE categories (7-11, 3-6, or 1-2 times/year) with no way to determine the correct target.

records.concept: Gender identity self-description (multi-select, check-all-that-apply: Man, Woman, Non-binary, Transgender, other)

cde.id: What terms best express how you describe your gender identity?

records.idealCde: Gender identity self-description: a multi-select (check-all-that-apply) question capturing how a person describes their own gender identity, with response options including Man, Woman, Non-binary, Transgender, and an open-text 'none of these / additional option' free-response field. The concept measures self-reported gender identity (distinct from biological sex assigned at birth and from sexual orientation). Answer type: categorical multi-select with optional free-text elaboration. Note: the source cluster also contains related but distinct fields — (1) biological sex assigned at birth (categorical: Male, Female, None of these + free text), (2) sexual orientation/sexuality (categorical: Heterosexual, Homosexual/Lesbian/Gay, Bisexual, with free-text 'something else' option), and (3) administratively recorded sex (binary categorical: Male, Female) — indicating that the ideal CDE set should treat gender identity, sex assigned at birth, and sexual orientation as separate, co-collected elements rather than collapsing them into one field.

transforms.targetCdeId: What terms best express how you describe your gender identity?
transforms.rationale: Man, Woman, Non-binary, and Prefer not to answer map cleanly. 'None of these describe me, and I'd like to consider additional options' aligns best with CDE code 96 ('None of these describe me'). 'GenderIdentity_Transgender' is ambiguous — the CDE distinguishes between Transgender man (FTM, code 3) and Transgender woman (MTF, code 4), so the undifferentiated 'Transgender' source code cannot be confidently mapped to either without additional information.

records.concept: Subject/participant biological sex (male/female)

cde.id: Sex type

records.idealCde: Gender identity self-description: a multi-select (check-all-that-apply) question capturing how a person describes their own gender identity, with response options including Man, Woman, Non-binary, Transgender, and an open-text 'none of these / additional option' free-response field. The concept measures self-reported gender identity (distinct from biological sex assigned at birth and from sexual orientation). Answer type: categorical multi-select with optional free-text elaboration. Note: the source cluster also contains related but distinct fields — (1) biological sex assigned at birth (categorical: Male, Female, None of these + free text), (2) sexual orientation/sexuality (categorical: Heterosexual, Homosexual/Lesbian/Gay, Bisexual, with free-text 'something else' option), and (3) administratively recorded sex (binary categorical: Male, Female) — indicating that the ideal CDE set should treat gender identity, sex assigned at birth, and sexual orientation as separate, co-collected elements rather than collapsing them into one field.

records.concept: Small-area income/multiple deprivation index score or rank (census geography unit)
records.idealCde: Small-area income deprivation score/rank: a numeric measure capturing the extent of income deprivation at the small-area level (e.g., Lower Super Output Area or equivalent census geography unit), answering 'What is the degree of income deprivation experienced by residents of this small area, based on the proportion of people living in households below an income threshold or claiming income-related benefits?' Answer type: continuous numeric score or rank (dimensionless index value or percentile rank within the national distribution), derived from composite administrative indicators including adults and children in income support, pension credit, and other low-income benefit households, as used in official small-area multiple deprivation indices (e.g., Welsh Index of Multiple Deprivation income domain, English Indices of Deprivation income domain). Qualifier: small-area geographic unit (not individual-level); values are area-level aggregates, not person-level measurements.

records.concept: Participant current marital/partner status

cde.id: Marital or partner status

records.idealCde: Current marital/partner status of the participant (self-report): a single categorical item asking 'What is your current marital/partner status?' with a closed enumerated response set covering at minimum: Married, Living with a partner/common-law relationship, Divorced, Separated, Widowed, Never married/never lived with a partner, and Prefer not to answer. Answer type: nominal categorical (single-select); no units. Captures the participant's own present legal and cohabiting partnership status at the time of data collection.

transforms.targetCdeId: Marital or partner status
transforms.rationale: numeric variable with no declared units — cannot author a unit conversion

transforms.targetCdeId: Marital or partner status
transforms.rationale: numeric variable with no declared units — cannot author a unit conversion

transforms.targetCdeId: Marital or partner status
transforms.rationale: Five source codes map directly by label. 'Living with partner' is mapped to 'Domestic partnership' as the closest CDE equivalent (cohabiting partner relationship), though it may not always imply a formal domestic partnership. 'Prefer not to answer' has no CDE equivalent and is a missing-data sentinel, so it is unmapped.

transforms.targetCdeId: Marital or partner status
transforms.rationale: Five source codes map directly by label. 'Living with partner' aligns well with 'Domestic partnership'. 'Prefer not to answer' is a missing-data sentinel with no equivalent CDE value.

transforms.targetCdeId: Marital or partner status
transforms.rationale: Source code 1 maps cleanly to 'Never married'. Source code 3,4,5 map directly. Source code 2 is ambiguous: 'Married/Living with a partner in a common-law relationship' conflates 'Married' and 'Domestic partnership' into one option; mapped to 'Married' as the primary term but this loses the domestic partnership distinction. No source codes are fully unmappable, but code 2 represents a meaningful loss of granularity.

transforms.targetCdeId: Marital or partner status
transforms.rationale: Source code 1 maps cleanly to 'Never married'. Source code 3,4,5 map directly. Source code 2 is ambiguous: 'Married/Living with a partner in a common-law relationship' conflates 'Married' and 'Domestic partnership' into one option; mapped to 'Married' as the primary term but this loses the domestic partnership distinction. No source codes are fully unmappable, but code 2 represents a meaningful loss of granularity.

records.concept: Spirometry contraindication status (any contraindication present)

cde.id: Spirometry Contraindicated

records.idealCde: Spirometry contraindication status: whether the participant has any contraindication to spirometry testing, defined as the presence of at least one of the following: chest infection in the last month (influenza, bronchitis, severe cold, pneumonia), history of detached retina, heart attack or surgery to eyes/chest/abdomen in the last 3 months, history of collapsed lung, pregnancy (1st or 3rd trimester), or current medication for tuberculosis. Question: 'Does the participant have any contraindication to spirometry?' Expected answer type: categorical single-select; values: Yes / Unsure / No.

transforms.targetCdeId: Spirometry Contraindicated
transforms.rationale: Source code 1 (Yes) maps directly to CDE 'Yes' and source code 0 (No) maps directly to CDE 'No'. Source code 2 (Unsure) has no equivalent in the binary Yes/No CDE and must remain unmapped.

records.concept: Difficulty walking alone up and down a flight of stairs (yes/no/unable/doctor's orders)

cde.id: How much DIFFICULTY do you currently have going up and down a flight of stairs inside, using a handrail?

records.idealCde: Functional limitation in mobility tasks: captures whether an individual experiences difficulty performing specific mobility activities (e.g., climbing a flight of stairs alone, walking 2–3 neighborhood blocks). The question asks: 'Do you have any difficulty performing [specified mobility task]?' Answer type is categorical/ordinal: 1=Yes (has difficulty) | 2=No (no difficulty) | 3=Unable to do (cannot perform the task) | 4=Don't do on doctor's orders; one response per distinct mobility task assessed.

transforms.targetCdeId: How much DIFFICULTY do you currently have going up and down a flight of stairs inside, using a handrail?
transforms.rationale: Source is a binary Yes/No format which doesn't align well with the CDE's 5-level difficulty scale. 'Yes' (able to do) maps loosely to 'No Difficulty' (5) and 'Unable to do' maps to 'Can't Do' (1). 'No' (source code 2) is ambiguous — it could imply difficulty or inability but cannot be confidently placed on the difficulty scale. 'Don't do on doctor's orders' (4) has no CDE equivalent and is left unmapped. Overall confidence is low due to the fundamental mismatch between binary and ordinal difficulty scales.

records.concept: Past-month electronic nicotine product use frequency (e-cigarettes, vape pens, hookah pens, personal vaporizers/mods, e-cigars, e-pipes, e-hookahs)

cde.id: E-cigarette electronic vapor product use status

records.idealCde: Current tobacco and electronic nicotine product use status and quantity in the past month: captures (1) use of electronic nicotine delivery systems (e-cigarettes, vape pens, hookah pens, personal vaporizers/mods, e-cigars, e-pipes, e-hookahs) with frequency response options (yes every day / yes some days / not currently but used in the past / never), and (2) current daily cigarette consumption as either an exact count (decimal number of cigarettes per day, with explicit capture for 26+ quantities) or an ordinal frequency band (1–5 / 6–10 / 11–15 / 16–20 / 21–25 / 26+ cigarettes per day). Time window: past month. Answer types: radio/ordinal for e-nicotine use frequency; decimal or ordinal categorical for cigarette count per day (units: cigarettes/day).

transforms.targetCdeId: E-cigarette electronic vapor product use status
transforms.rationale: COPE_A_197 ('Yes, every day') maps directly to 'Every day' and COPE_A_169 ('Yes, some days') maps directly to 'Some days'. COPE_A_94 ('Not currently, but in the past') and COPE_A_131 ('No, never') both represent non-current use but neither aligns precisely with 'Not at all' (which typically means never/not currently without distinguishing past use). The CDE lacks a 'former user' category, and collapsing past use and never-use into 'Not at all' would lose meaningful clinical distinction, so these are left unmapped rather than forced.

records.concept: Self-reported overall/global general health status (excellent to poor)

cde.id: In general, would you say your overall health is [PhenX]

records.idealCde: Self-reported general health status: a single-item self-assessment capturing the respondent's overall perceived health (physical, mental, or social functioning depending on domain variant). The concept encompasses three related but distinct sub-domains present in the source fields — (1) overall/global general health ('In general, would you say your health is excellent, very good, good, fair, or poor?'), (2) general mental health including mood and ability to think, and (3) general social role functioning including home, work, and community activities. Each sub-domain is answered by the participant about themselves using a 5-point ordinal Likert-type scale: Excellent / Very Good / Good / Fair / Poor. Answer type: single-select categorical ordinal; 5 response options (Excellent, Very Good, Good, Fair, Poor). No physical units. Self-report; no qualifier for laterality or body site. Note: the source fields represent three distinct self-rated health constructs (global health, mental health, social functioning) that should ideally be captured as separate CDEs within an overall health perception module rather than collapsed into one.

transforms.targetCdeId: In general, would you say your overall health is [PhenX]
transforms.rationale: numeric variable with no declared units — cannot author a unit conversion

transforms.targetCdeId: In general, would you say your overall health is [PhenX]
transforms.rationale: Perfect 1-to-1 match between source and CDE values; all five response options align exactly in both code and meaning.

transforms.targetCdeId: In general, would you say your overall health is [PhenX]
transforms.rationale: Perfect 1-to-1 match between source and CDE values; all five response options align exactly in both code and meaning.

transforms.targetCdeId: In general, would you say your overall health is [PhenX]
transforms.rationale: Source code 1 (Excellent) maps cleanly to CDE 1 (Excellent). Source codes 3 (Fair) and 4 (Poor) map to CDE 4 and 5 respectively. Source code 2 is 'Good' which could map to CDE 2 (Very Good) or CDE 3 (Good) — since the source skips 'Very Good' entirely and uses only a 4-point scale (Excellent/Good/Fair/Poor), 'Good' in the source likely corresponds to a collapsed middle category that doesn't cleanly align with either CDE 2 or CDE 3, so it is left unmapped to avoid ambiguity. Source codes -1 and -3 are missing-data sentinels with no CDE equivalent.

cde.id: In general, how would you rate your mental health, including your mood and your ability to think?

records.idealCde: Self-reported general health status: a single-item self-assessment capturing the respondent's overall perceived health (physical, mental, or social functioning depending on domain variant). The concept encompasses three related but distinct sub-domains present in the source fields — (1) overall/global general health ('In general, would you say your health is excellent, very good, good, fair, or poor?'), (2) general mental health including mood and ability to think, and (3) general social role functioning including home, work, and community activities. Each sub-domain is answered by the participant about themselves using a 5-point ordinal Likert-type scale: Excellent / Very Good / Good / Fair / Poor. Answer type: single-select categorical ordinal; 5 response options (Excellent, Very Good, Good, Fair, Poor). No physical units. Self-report; no qualifier for laterality or body site. Note: the source fields represent three distinct self-rated health constructs (global health, mental health, social functioning) that should ideally be captured as separate CDEs within an overall health perception module rather than collapsed into one.

transforms.targetCdeId: In general, how would you rate your mental health, including your mood and your ability to think?

records.concept: Depression symptom — hopefulness about the future frequency (past week), CES-D style 4-point scale

cde.id: Center for Epidemiologic Studies-Depression Scale (CES-D) - hopeful future indicator

records.idealCde: Depression symptom — hopefulness about the future frequency (past week): Captures how often the respondent felt hopeful about the future during the past week (reference window implied by 5-7 day maximum). Single-item self-report. Answer type: ordinal categorical — 1=All of the time (5–7 days), 2=Occasionally (3–4 days), 3=Some of the time (1–2 days), 4=Rarely or never (less than 1 day).

transforms.targetCdeId: Center for Epidemiologic Studies-Depression Scale (CES-D) - hopeful future indicator
transforms.rationale: The source uses a reversed 1-4 scale (All of the time=1 down to Rarely/never=4) while the CDE uses 0-3 (Rarely=0 up to Most/all=3). The frequency labels match precisely when reversed: source 1->CDE 3, source 2->CDE 2, source 3->CDE 1, source 4->CDE 0. Note that this item is typically positively worded (hopeful future) on the CES-D and is reverse-scored relative to depressive symptoms, but the mapping here is purely based on frequency label alignment.

records.concept: Self-perceived body weight status (overweight / underweight / just about right)

cde.id: Self-perception of body weight

records.idealCde: Self-reported body weight perception and anthropometric status: captures the respondent's own assessment of their weight status and associated body mass index metrics derived from self-reported measurements. Question answered: 'How does the respondent perceive their own weight, and what is their self-reported body weight (kg) and calculated BMI?' Answer type: (1) Categorical weight perception — ordinal text (Underweight / Just about right / Overweight); (2) Self-reported body weight in kilograms — continuous decimal (kg); (3) Self-reported BMI — continuous decimal (kg/m²); (4) BMI classification per International Standard based on self-reported data — ordinal text (Underweight / Normal / Overweight / Obese class I / Obese class II / Obese class III). All measures are self-reported by the subject (not clinician-measured). Source concept spans both subjective weight perception and objective-equivalent anthropometric indices derived from self-report.

transforms.targetCdeId: Self-perception of body weight
transforms.rationale: Source codes 1 (Overweight) and 2 (Underweight) lack degree/intensity information, so they are mapped to the closest moderate CDE equivalents ('Slightly overweight' and 'Slightly underweight'). This is a best-effort mapping since the source does not distinguish between 'slightly' and 'very' overweight/underweight. Code 3 maps cleanly to 'About the right weight'.

records.idealCde: Self-reported body weight perception and anthropometric status: captures the respondent's own assessment of their weight status and associated body mass index metrics derived from self-reported measurements. Question answered: 'How does the respondent perceive their own weight, and what is their self-reported body weight (kg) and calculated BMI?' Answer type: (1) Categorical weight perception — ordinal text (Underweight / Just about right / Overweight); (2) Self-reported body weight in kilograms — continuous decimal (kg); (3) Self-reported BMI — continuous decimal (kg/m²); (4) BMI classification per International Standard based on self-reported data — ordinal text (Underweight / Normal / Overweight / Obese class I / Obese class II / Obese class III). All measures are self-reported by the subject (not clinician-measured). Source concept spans both subjective weight perception and objective-equivalent anthropometric indices derived from self-report.

records.idealCde: Self-reported body weight perception and anthropometric status: captures the respondent's own assessment of their weight status and associated body mass index metrics derived from self-reported measurements. Question answered: 'How does the respondent perceive their own weight, and what is their self-reported body weight (kg) and calculated BMI?' Answer type: (1) Categorical weight perception — ordinal text (Underweight / Just about right / Overweight); (2) Self-reported body weight in kilograms — continuous decimal (kg); (3) Self-reported BMI — continuous decimal (kg/m²); (4) BMI classification per International Standard based on self-reported data — ordinal text (Underweight / Normal / Overweight / Obese class I / Obese class II / Obese class III). All measures are self-reported by the subject (not clinician-measured). Source concept spans both subjective weight perception and objective-equivalent anthropometric indices derived from self-report.

transforms.targetCdeId: Measurement not reported reason
transforms.rationale: The CDE offers only two values: 'Not applicable' and 'Unknown'. 'Reason not known' (300) and 'Reason not known' (506) map to 'Unknown' as they explicitly indicate the reason is not known. All other source codes represent specific, known reasons (participant refusal, equipment issues, health conditions, nurse advice, etc.) that do not align with either 'Not applicable' or 'Unknown' — they are distinct, identifiable reasons with no matching CDE code. 'Not applicable' would imply the measurement was not attempted for a reason outside the scope of the question, but none of the source codes clearly convey that meaning. Confidence is moderate because the CDE is very coarse relative to the rich source taxonomy.

records.concept: Permanent menstrual cessation status (have periods stopped permanently, with options: no/yes-none/yes-hormone-induced/not sure)

cde.id: Have your menstrual periods stopped permanently [PhenX]

records.idealCde: Permanent menstrual cessation status: Asks whether the respondent's menstrual periods have stopped permanently. Answer type is a single-select categorical response with options: (1) No, periods have not stopped; (2) Yes, no menstrual periods at all; (3) Yes, but periods are induced by hormones; (4) Not sure. Captures self-reported menopausal/amenorrhea status in females, distinguishing natural cessation from hormone-induced bleeding and uncertainty.

transforms.targetCdeId: Have your menstrual periods stopped permanently [PhenX]
transforms.rationale: Four source codes map directly and unambiguously to the four CDE values by meaning. PMI_PreferNotToAnswer has no equivalent in the CDE value set and is left unmapped.

records.concept: Vigorous physical activity participation in the past 7 days (Yes/No, ≥10 min at a time)

cde.id: In the past 7 days, How many days did you exercise <u>really hard</u> for 10 minutes or more?

records.idealCde: Physical activity participation in the past 7 days: A set of binary and frequency measures capturing whether an individual engaged in specific types of physical activity during the preceding 7-day period, including (1) vigorous-intensity aerobic activities (e.g., heavy lifting, digging, aerobics, fast bicycling) performed for at least 10 continuous minutes — answered Yes/No; (2) walking for at least 10 continuous minutes at a time across any domain (work, home, transport, leisure) — answered Yes/No; and (3) muscle-strengthening and endurance exercises (e.g., weight lifting, push-ups) — answered as a frequency count or ordinal rating over the past 7 days. Time window qualifier: last 7 days. Answer types: dichotomous Yes/No for aerobic and walking items; numeric frequency (days per week or times per week) or free-text for muscle-strengthening item.

transforms.targetCdeId: In the past 7 days, How many days did you exercise <u>really hard</u> for 10 minutes or more?
transforms.rationale: The source variable appears to be a binary Yes/No item (COPE_A_44=Yes, COPE_A_13=No), likely from a COPE coping inventory, which does not align with the frequency-of-vigorous-exercise scale (No days through 6-7 days) of the target CDE. No meaningful mapping can be made without distorting the meaning of either variable.

records.concept: Frequency of muscle-strengthening exercise in the past 7 days (e.g., weight lifting, push-ups)
records.idealCde: Physical activity participation in the past 7 days: A set of binary and frequency measures capturing whether an individual engaged in specific types of physical activity during the preceding 7-day period, including (1) vigorous-intensity aerobic activities (e.g., heavy lifting, digging, aerobics, fast bicycling) performed for at least 10 continuous minutes — answered Yes/No; (2) walking for at least 10 continuous minutes at a time across any domain (work, home, transport, leisure) — answered Yes/No; and (3) muscle-strengthening and endurance exercises (e.g., weight lifting, push-ups) — answered as a frequency count or ordinal rating over the past 7 days. Time window qualifier: last 7 days. Answer types: dichotomous Yes/No for aerobic and walking items; numeric frequency (days per week or times per week) or free-text for muscle-strengthening item.

records.concept: Dietary frequency of butter or regular margarine on bread or cooked vegetables (frequency unit category + numeric count)
records.idealCde: Dietary frequency of butter or regular margarine consumption on bread or cooked vegetables: captures how often an individual usually eats butter or regular margarine on bread or on cooked vegetables only, expressed as a two-part composite answer consisting of (1) a frequency unit category (Rarely/Never, Per Day, Per Week, Per Month, Per Year) and (2) a corresponding numeric quantity (decimal count per the selected time unit). Note: the source concept cluster also includes a parallel numeric frequency field for 'other vegetables (except carrots, potatoes, or salad)', indicating the cluster may conflate two distinct dietary frequency items — butter/margarine on bread or cooked vegetables and other vegetable intake — which should ideally be captured as separate CDEs: one for butter/margarine frequency (frequency unit + numeric amount) and one for other vegetable consumption frequency (numeric amount per time unit).

records.concept: Self-reported ethnic background with hierarchical branching categories (e.g., White/British/Irish, Mixed, Asian or Asian British, Black or Black British, Other)

records.idealCde: Ethnic background: The self-reported ethnic or racial group with which a participant identifies, captured via a branching categorical question administered at initial assessment. Question: 'What is your ethnic background?' Answer type: Single-select categorical with hierarchical branching (top-level groups such as White, Mixed, Asian or Asian British, Black or Black British, Other; with sub-categories such as British, Irish, Any other White background, etc.). Collected from the participant themselves (self-report).

records.concept: Doctor-diagnosed heart attack or myocardial infarction (ever told, binary Yes/No)

cde.id: Ever told by doctor that you had a myocardial infarction or heart attack

records.idealCde: Physician-diagnosed cardiovascular condition (self-reported): A binary indicator capturing whether a doctor has ever told the respondent that they have a specific cardiovascular condition. The question asked is: 'Has a doctor ever told you that you have [condition]?' where [condition] is one of: heart attack or myocardial infarction (AMI), angina or chest pain due to heart disease, or heart disease including congestive heart failure (CHF). Each condition is captured as a separate item. Answer type: categorical, 1=Yes / 2=No. Note: member fields carry two distinct qualifier contexts — community-dwelling participants (suffix 'com') and terminated/deceased participants (suffix 'trm') — indicating the same cardiovascular condition questions were administered across both subpopulations; an ideal CDE set should preserve this participant-status qualifier as a separate dimension rather than collapsing across groups.

transforms.targetCdeId: Ever told by doctor that you had a myocardial infarction or heart attack
transforms.rationale: Direct mapping: source 1=Yes maps to CDE 1=Yes, source 2=No maps to CDE 0=No. No missing-data or don't-know sentinel in source.

transforms.targetCdeId: Ever told by doctor that you had a myocardial infarction or heart attack

records.concept: Doctor-diagnosed heart disease including congestive heart failure/CHF (ever told, binary Yes/No)

cde.id: Has doctor ever told you that you had heart trouble [PhenX]

records.idealCde: Physician-diagnosed cardiovascular condition (self-reported): A binary indicator capturing whether a doctor has ever told the respondent that they have a specific cardiovascular condition. The question asked is: 'Has a doctor ever told you that you have [condition]?' where [condition] is one of: heart attack or myocardial infarction (AMI), angina or chest pain due to heart disease, or heart disease including congestive heart failure (CHF). Each condition is captured as a separate item. Answer type: categorical, 1=Yes / 2=No. Note: member fields carry two distinct qualifier contexts — community-dwelling participants (suffix 'com') and terminated/deceased participants (suffix 'trm') — indicating the same cardiovascular condition questions were administered across both subpopulations; an ideal CDE set should preserve this participant-status qualifier as a separate dimension rather than collapsing across groups.

records.concept: Self-reported moderate physical activity participation in last 7 days (yes/no)

cde.id: Regular moderate intense aerobic exercise physical activity indicator

records.idealCde: Moderate-to-vigorous physical activity (MVPA) guideline attainment: Captures whether an individual meets recommended thresholds for moderate-to-vigorous physical activity based on self-reported activity over the past 7 days, specifically whether they achieved at least 150 minutes per week of moderate-intensity activity (or 75 minutes of vigorous-intensity activity, or an equivalent combination). The CDE answers the question: 'Did this person meet the recommended weekly moderate-to-vigorous physical activity guidelines?' Answer type: Binary categorical (Yes / No). Note: member fields carry two distinct qualifiers — one excludes walking from the moderate activity calculation and one includes walking toward the moderate activity threshold — reflecting different operationalizations of the same guideline; an ideal implementation should specify which definition applies (walking-inclusive vs. walking-exclusive) or capture both as separate sub-elements.

transforms.targetCdeId: Regular moderate intense aerobic exercise physical activity indicator
transforms.rationale: COPE_A_44 maps to 'Yes' and COPE_A_13 maps to 'No' based on the explicit Yes/No labels provided with each source code. No 'Unknown' option exists in the source, so that CDE value remains unused. Confidence is slightly reduced because the COPE item labels alone (without full question context) are assumed to directly correspond to the CDE's Yes/No values.

records.concept: Clinician-diagnosed circulatory/cardiovascular conditions (multi-select, self-reported, ever told by doctor)

cde.id: Health Conditions - Disease Disorders

records.idealCde: Cholesterol measurement concept capturing multiple distinct aspects of cholesterol across different contexts and qualifiers: (1) clinician-diagnosed circulatory/cardiovascular conditions (self-reported, ever diagnosed by a doctor), answered as multi-select checkbox including conditions such as anemia, atrial fibrillation, bleeding disorder, and congestive heart failure; (2) blood cholesterol laboratory value — a quantitative measurement of total cholesterol concentration in blood, answering 'What is the patient's blood cholesterol level?', expressed as a decimal numeric value in mmol/L, with an associated free-text comment field for contextual annotation; and (3) dietary cholesterol intake estimated from a 24-hour dietary recall, answering 'How much cholesterol was consumed in the past 24 hours?', expressed as a continuous numeric value in mg. Note: member fields carry different qualifiers (clinical diagnosis history vs. blood biomarker vs. dietary intake) and should ideally be represented as separate CDEs rather than collapsed into one.

transforms.targetCdeId: Health Conditions - Disease Disorders
transforms.rationale: Clear matches exist for heart failure, coronary heart disease, high cholesterol, hypertension, sickle cell disease, stroke, and thrombotic disorders (pulmonary embolism/DVT). TIA is mapped to Stroke as the closest CDE equivalent. 'Other heart or blood condition' maps to 'Other chronic diseases/specify' and 'No heart or blood condition' maps to 'None of the above'. Anemia, atrial fibrillation, bleeding disorder, heart attack (distinct from coronary heart disease but no specific CDE code), heart valve disease, and peripheral vascular disease have no direct CDE equivalents and are left unmapped. Heart attack could arguably map to Coronary heart disease but the distinction is clinically meaningful enough to leave unmapped.

records.idealCde: Cholesterol measurement concept capturing multiple distinct aspects of cholesterol across different contexts and qualifiers: (1) clinician-diagnosed circulatory/cardiovascular conditions (self-reported, ever diagnosed by a doctor), answered as multi-select checkbox including conditions such as anemia, atrial fibrillation, bleeding disorder, and congestive heart failure; (2) blood cholesterol laboratory value — a quantitative measurement of total cholesterol concentration in blood, answering 'What is the patient's blood cholesterol level?', expressed as a decimal numeric value in mmol/L, with an associated free-text comment field for contextual annotation; and (3) dietary cholesterol intake estimated from a 24-hour dietary recall, answering 'How much cholesterol was consumed in the past 24 hours?', expressed as a continuous numeric value in mg. Note: member fields carry different qualifiers (clinical diagnosis history vs. blood biomarker vs. dietary intake) and should ideally be represented as separate CDEs rather than collapsed into one.

records.concept: Has a doctor ever told you that you had cancer (Yes/No)

cde.id: Have you ever had cancer PhenX

records.idealCde: Self-reported lifetime cancer diagnosis history: captures (1) whether a physician has ever told the respondent they have/had cancer (binary Yes/No), and if yes, (2) which specific cancer type(s) were diagnosed, recorded as individual binary indicators per cancer site/type (e.g., bladder, breast, colorectal, small intestine, and others). Concept: ever-diagnosis of cancer by a doctor, by cancer type. Question answered: 'Has a doctor ever told you that you had cancer, and if so, what type(s)?' Answer type: top-level response is a binary categorical (1=Yes, 2=No); cancer-type sub-items are binary flags (1=Yes, 0=No) per cancer site, covering at minimum bladder, breast, colorectal/colon, and small intestine/bowel, with provision for additional site-specific indicators. No laterality, body-side, or time-window qualifier is specified; qualifier is lifetime ('ever') self-report.

transforms.targetCdeId: Have you ever had cancer PhenX
transforms.rationale: Direct 1:1 mapping between source codes and CDE values. Source code 1=Yes maps to CDE 'Yes', source code 2=No maps to CDE 'No'.

records.concept: Contact with health care provider (various types) about physical or mental health in past 12 months

cde.id: Medical office visit past year indicator

records.idealCde: Health care utilization and treatment contact for physical or mental health concerns over the past 12 months, covering multiple provider types (general practitioner/family physician, physiotherapist/occupational therapist/chiropractor, psychologist, and medical specialist such as cardiologist/gynaecologist/psychiatrist), plus whether a medication was ever prescribed by a health professional specifically for unusual or psychotic experiences (seeing visions, hearing voices, believing something strange was communicating with them, or believing there was a plot against them — asked only when at least one such experience was endorsed). The CDE captures: (1) binary yes/no contact with each named provider type in the past 12 months, and (2) binary yes/no ever-prescribed medication for psychotic/unusual experiences (conditional on endorsement of at least one such experience). Answer type: categorical (Yes / No) for each provider-contact item and for the medication-prescription item; time window for provider contact is past 12 months; time window for medication prescription is lifetime ('ever').

records.concept: Waist circumference in centimeters

records.concept: Non-cancer illness classification code (coded representation of participant-reported non-cancer illness, derived via coding tree by trained nurse/interviewer, with free-text fallback reviewed by physician)
records.idealCde: Non-cancer illness classification code: captures the coded representation of a non-cancer illness reported by a participant, derived through a structured coding tree applied by a trained interviewer (nurse); when the illness cannot be located in the coding tree, a free-text description is recorded and subsequently reviewed by a physician for coding where possible. Question answered: 'What non-cancer illness has the participant reported, and what is its assigned classification code?' Answer type: categorical code (from a defined illness coding tree/taxonomy) with an optional free-text fallback field for uncodeable conditions; units: coded illness identifier (alphanumeric code) or free-text string.

records.concept: Family history of diabetes (Type 1, Type 2, Other) by biological relative

cde.id: Family health problems endocrine metabolic history type

records.idealCde: Family history and personal prediabetes status related to diabetes conditions (endocrine): A composite concept capturing two distinct but related diabetes-domain elements. (1) Family history of diabetes by type and biological relative: for each diabetes subtype (Type 1 diabetes, Type 2 diabetes, Other diabetes), whether a first-degree biological relative (mother, father, sibling, daughter, son) has been diagnosed — answered as a multi-select checkbox per relative per diabetes type; expected answer type: categorical multi-select (relative role × diabetes type combinations, e.g., Mother/Type1Diabetes, Father/Type2Diabetes, etc.). (2) Personal prediabetes diagnosis — current status: whether the participant currently has prediabetes (Yes/No radio); and age of onset life stage at prediabetes diagnosis: ordinal categorical radio with values Child (0–11), Adolescent (12–17), Adult (18–64), Older Adult (65–74), Elderly (75+). Note: member fields carry different qualifiers — family-history fields are qualified by biological relative and diabetes subtype (third-party/family context), while prediabetes fields are self-report (participant/self context); these should be treated as separate CDEs if decomposed.

transforms.targetCdeId: Family health problems endocrine metabolic history type
transforms.rationale: All source codes represent Type 1 Diabetes diagnoses in various family members (Mother, Father, Sibling, Daughter, Son, Grandparent). They all map cleanly to the CDE value 'Diabetes (indicate if type I, type II)'. The family member role encoded in the source variable name is metadata about the relationship, not the condition type, so it does not affect the condition mapping. High confidence because the condition is explicitly named and directly corresponds to the CDE diabetes category.

transforms.targetCdeId: Family health problems endocrine metabolic history type
transforms.rationale: All source codes represent Type 2 Diabetes diagnoses in various family members (Mother, Father, Sibling, Daughter, Son, Grandparent). These all map cleanly to the CDE value 'Diabetes (indicate if type I, type II)'. The source codes specify Type 2, which aligns with the CDE's parenthetical note about indicating diabetes type. The family member qualifiers (Mother, Father, etc.) are relational attributes of the record rather than distinct answer values, so they do not prevent mapping. Confidence is slightly below 1.0 because the CDE is a single field that covers both Type I and Type II, while the source specifically encodes Type 2; downstream users should note the type distinction.

transforms.targetCdeId: Family health problems endocrine metabolic history type
transforms.rationale: All source codes share the suffix 'OtherDiabetes' and differ only by family member role (Mother, Father, Sibling, Daughter, Son, Grandparent). The CDE captures the condition type, not the family member relationship, so all map to 'Diabetes (indicate if type I, type II)'. Confidence is slightly reduced because 'OtherDiabetes' may imply a diabetes subtype not clearly type I or type II (e.g., gestational, MODY), but diabetes is still the best-fitting CDE value available.

transforms.targetCdeId: Celiac disease gluten sensitivity diagnosis ever indicator
transforms.rationale: The source variable asks about prescribed medications for Celiac Disease, which implies a diagnosis of celiac disease/gluten sensitivity. 'Yes' to Rx meds for celiac maps to 'Yes' for ever diagnosed, and 'No' maps to 'No'. However, confidence is moderate because having/not having Rx meds is not identical to ever having a diagnosis — a patient could be diagnosed but not prescribed medications, or the 'No' could mean no meds rather than no diagnosis. The mapping is directionally reasonable but not a perfect semantic match.

records.idealCde: Self-reported change in body weight over the past 6 months: a two-part CDE capturing (1) the direction of weight change — whether the respondent gained weight, lost weight, or stayed about the same compared to 6 months ago (categorical: gained / lost / stayed the same), and (2) the magnitude of that change — how many pounds or kilograms were gained or lost in the past 6 months (numeric, units: pounds or kilograms). Answers the question: 'Compared with 6 months ago, have you gained weight, lost weight, or stayed about the same, and if so, by how much?'

transforms.targetCdeId: Body weight change goal
transforms.rationale: numeric variable with no declared units — cannot author a unit conversion

records.concept: Family psychiatric and substance use disorder history (blood relatives) — multi-select checkbox array of specific mental health/substance use conditions

cde.id: Family health problems psychological history type

records.idealCde: Family psychiatric and substance use disorder history (blood relatives): Captures whether any biological family member has ever been diagnosed with specific mental health or substance use conditions. Question asked: 'Has anyone in your family ever been diagnosed with the following mental health or substance use conditions? Think only of people you are related to by blood.' Answer type: Multi-select checkbox array; each item is a distinct condition (e.g., Alcohol use disorder, Anxiety/panic disorder, Autism spectrum disorder, Bipolar disorder, and additional conditions in the full list); response per condition is binary presence/absence (yes selected vs. not selected), with the full set of responses representing a profile of family psychiatric history. Qualifier: biological/blood relatives only; no restriction on specific relative type or generation; lifetime diagnosis timeframe.

transforms.targetCdeId: Family health problems psychological history type
transforms.rationale: Alcohol use disorder and drug use disorder both map to 'Substance abuse (alcohol, drugs)'. Anxiety reaction/panic disorder maps cleanly to 'Anxiety/panic disorder'. Bipolar disorder maps to 'Bipolar disorder/manic depressive disorder'. Depression maps directly. Schizophrenia is a psychotic disorder and maps to 'Other type of psychosis' as the closest available CDE option. Autism spectrum disorder has no equivalent in the CDE value set. PMI_None, PMI_DontKnow, and PMI_PreferNotToAnswer are administrative/missing-data sentinels with no CDE equivalent.

records.concept: Participant's highest level of educational attainment (self-reported), spanning grade-level schooling through post-secondary degrees/diplomas

cde.id: What is the highest grade or level of school you have completed or the highest degree you have received [NHANES]

records.idealCde: Educational attainment (self-reported): What is the highest level of education (grade, diploma, degree, or certificate) the participant has ever completed? Ordinal categorical variable capturing the full spectrum from no formal schooling through advanced post-secondary credentials. Answer type: single-select ordinal category from a standardized scale spanning (1) never attended/kindergarten only, (2) grades 1–4 (primary), (3) grades 5–8 (middle/junior), (4) grades 9–10 (some high school), (5) grades 11–13/high school diploma or equivalent, (6) trade/vocational certificate or diploma, (7) non-university post-secondary certificate or diploma (e.g., community college, CEGEP, nursing school), (8) bachelor's degree, (9) graduate or professional degree (master's, doctorate, professional). No laterality, body-site, or condition qualifier; subject is the participant (self).

transforms.targetCdeId: What is the highest grade or level of school you have completed or the highest degree you have received [NHANES]
transforms.rationale: numeric variable with no declared units — cannot author a unit conversion

transforms.targetCdeId: What is the highest grade or level of school you have completed or the highest degree you have received [NHANES]
transforms.rationale: Several source codes span multiple CDE grades/levels and cannot be mapped to a single CDE code without ambiguity. HighestGrade_NeverAttended maps cleanly to 0. HighestGrade_TwelveOrGED covers both code 13 (HS graduate) and 14 (GED); mapped to 13 as the label says 'High school graduate' but this is imprecise. HighestGrade_CollegeGraduate maps to 18 (Bachelor's). PMI_PreferNotToAnswer maps to 77 (Refused). HighestGrade_OneThroughFour spans codes 1-4, HighestGrade_FiveThroughEight spans 5-8, HighestGrade_NineThroughEleven spans 9-11, HighestGrade_CollegeOnetoThree spans 15-17, and HighestGrade_AdvancedDegree spans 19-21 — none of these can be mapped to a single CDE code without loss of information.

transforms.targetCdeId: What is the highest grade or level of school you have completed or the highest degree you have received [NHANES]
transforms.rationale: Source codes represent broad grade ranges mapped to the highest grade within each range: code 1 ('Grade 8 or lower') maps to CDE 8 (8th grade) as the upper bound; code 2 ('Grade 9-10') maps to CDE 10 (10th grade) as the upper bound; code 3 ('Grade 11-13') maps to CDE 12 (12th grade, no diploma) as the closest match — CDE only goes to grade 12 and code 13 is 'High school graduate', but grade 13 in some Canadian provinces is a pre-university year that doesn't cleanly map to a US diploma. Confidence is moderate because these are ranges being mapped to single grade points, and the Canadian grading system (especially Quebec's secondary system) does not perfectly align with US grade levels.

transforms.targetCdeId: What is the highest grade or level of school you have completed or the highest degree you have received [NHANES]
transforms.rationale: Source is a Canadian post-secondary education classification (likely Statistics Canada). '01' (no post-secondary degree) could map to many CDE levels (0-14) but we cannot determine which grade/diploma level without more info. '04' (university certificate below bachelor's) has no clean CDE equivalent — closest might be 15 (some college, no degree) but it implies a certificate was earned, not just attendance. '06' (above bachelor's) is ambiguous — could be master's (19), professional (20), or doctoral (21). '02' mapped to 16 (occupational/vocational associate) and '03' to 17 (academic associate/community college) as best approximations, though these are imperfect Canadian-to-US mappings. '97' (Other) has no CDE equivalent. Confidence is moderate-low due to the Canadian vs. US education system mismatch and the coarse granularity of the source.

transforms.targetCdeId: What is the highest grade or level of school you have completed or the highest degree you have received [NHANES]
transforms.rationale: The source variable is a binary Yes/No question, which has no meaningful correspondence to any of the educational attainment grade/degree levels in the target CDE. No valid mapping is possible.

transforms.targetCdeId: What is the highest grade or level of school you have completed or the highest degree you have received [NHANES]
transforms.rationale: The source variable is a binary Yes/No question, which has no meaningful correspondence to any of the educational attainment grade/degree levels in the target CDE. No valid mapping is possible.

records.concept: Lifetime cigarette smoking initiation (ever smoked at least 100 cigarettes or a whole cigarette)

cde.id: Tobacco smoked 100 cigarettes indicator

records.idealCde: Lifetime tobacco smoking initiation (self-reported): A yes/no question asking whether the respondent has ever smoked tobacco products (cigarettes, cigars, cigarillos, filtered cigars, hookah, or other combustible tobacco) at a meaningful threshold of use (e.g., at least 100 cigarettes in their lifetime, or at least one whole cigarette, or even one or two puffs of any combustible tobacco product). Captures any history of combustible tobacco use by the participant themselves. Answer type: categorical — Yes / No / Don't know / Prefer not to answer. Note: member fields vary in the specific threshold applied (100 lifetime cigarettes vs. ever a whole cigarette vs. ever even one or two puffs) and in the tobacco product type assessed (cigarettes only vs. cigars/cigarillos vs. hookah); an ideal unified CDE should specify a single consistent threshold and product scope, or be split into product-specific items each with a defined minimum-use threshold.

transforms.targetCdeId: Tobacco smoked 100 cigarettes indicator
transforms.rationale: Yes/No map directly. 'Don't know' aligns well with 'Unknown'. 'Prefer not to answer' is a refusal/non-response sentinel with no equivalent CDE code — it is not the same as Unknown and cannot be confidently mapped.

transforms.targetCdeId: Tobacco smoked 100 cigarettes indicator
transforms.rationale: Direct 1:1 mapping; Yes/No align perfectly. No unknown/missing sentinel in source.

Note: member fields vary in the specific threshold applied (100 lifetime cigarettes vs. ever a whole cigarette vs. ever even one or two puffs) and in the tobacco product type assessed (cigarettes only vs. cigars/cigarillos vs. hookah); an ideal unified CDE should specify a single consistent threshold and product scope, or be split into product-specific items each with a defined minimum-use threshold.

transforms.targetCdeId: Former smoker 6Mo PhenX

records.concept: Prescribed medication for a specified circulatory condition (Anemia, Hypertension, Peripheral Vascular Disease, Pulmonary Embolism, Sickle Cell Disease)
records.idealCde: Circulatory condition medication prescription status: For a specified circulatory condition (e.g., anemia, hypertension, peripheral vascular disease) diagnosed in the respondent, captures whether the respondent has been prescribed medication to treat that condition. Question: 'Have you been prescribed medication for [circulatory condition]?' Answer type: Categorical (radio); values: Yes / No. Note: Member fields also capture hypertension-specific sub-elements including (a) whether hypertension is currently active (Yes/No) and (b) age of onset life stage at hypertension diagnosis (Child [0–11] / Adolescent [12–17] / Adult [18–64] / Older adult [65–74] / Older adult [75+]); these represent distinct but related CDEs within the same circulatory conditions module, each conditioned on the respondent having the specified circulatory condition.

transforms.targetCdeId: Past current cardiovascular illness type
transforms.rationale: The 'Yes' response for current hypertension maps directly to the CDE value for high blood pressure/hypertension. The 'No' response indicates absence of the condition and has no corresponding CDE permissible value (the CDE lists condition types, not negations).

transforms.targetCdeId: Past current cardiovascular illness type
transforms.rationale: The 'Yes' response to congestive heart failure currently maps clearly to 'Heart failure' in the CDE. The 'No' response indicates absence of the condition and has no corresponding CDE permissible value (the CDE lists condition types, not presence/absence indicators), so it is unmapped.

transforms.targetCdeId: Past current cardiovascular illness type
transforms.rationale: The source variable appears to be a yes/no indicator for current coronary artery disease. 'Yes' maps to the CDE value covering coronary artery disease. records.concept: Chest pain or discomfort presence and characteristics (general screen, during/outside activity, relieved by standing, walking pace/uphill)

cde.id: Any chest discomfort ever/since last exam or medical history update?

records.idealCde: Chest pain or discomfort presence and characteristics: a set of related items assessing (1) whether the individual ever experiences pain or discomfort in the chest (general screen; categorical: Yes / No / Do not know / Prefer not to answer), (2) whether chest pain occurs during physical activity (categorical: Yes / Unsure / No), (3) whether chest pain occurs at rest or outside of physical activity in the past month (categorical: Yes / Unsure / No), (4) whether chest pain is relieved by standing still (categorical: Yes / No / Do not know), and (5) whether the individual is able to walk or cycle unaided for 10 minutes as a functional exercise-capacity safety screen (categorical: Yes / Unsure / No). Together these items characterize the presence, exertional vs. rest pattern, and relief characteristics of chest pain/discomfort in the self-reporting participant, supporting identification of anginal symptom patterns and pre-exercise safety screening. All items are self-reported by the participant; answer type is categorical single-select per item.

transforms.sourceVariable: UKBB:Chest pain due to walking ceases when standing still
transforms.targetCdeId: Any chest discomfort ever/since last exam or medical history update?
transforms.rationale: Yes->1 and No->0 are direct matches. 'Do not know' maps to CDE code 9 (Don't know). '-3' (Prefer not to answer) has no equivalent in the CDE value set and cannot be mapped. CDE code 2 (Maybe) has no source equivalent.

transforms.targetCdeId: Any chest discomfort ever/since last exam or medical history update?
transforms.rationale: Yes->1 and No->0 are direct matches. 'Do not know' maps to CDE code 9 (Don't know). '-3' (Prefer not to answer) has no equivalent in the CDE value set and cannot be mapped. CDE code 2 (Maybe) has no source equivalent.

transforms.sourceVariable: UKBB:Chest pain felt during physical activity
transforms.targetCdeId: Any chest discomfort ever/since last exam or medical history update?
transforms.rationale: Direct alignment: source Yes(1)->CDE Yes(1), source No(0)->CDE No(0), source Unsure(2)->CDE Maybe(2). CDE code 9 (Don't know) has no source equivalent but that is fine as it simply goes unused.

transforms.sourceVariable: UKBB:Chest pain felt outside physical activity
transforms.targetCdeId: Any chest discomfort ever/since last exam or medical history update?
transforms.rationale: Direct alignment: source Yes(1)->CDE Yes(1), source No(0)->CDE No(0), source Unsure(2)->CDE Maybe(2). CDE code 9 (Don't know) has no source equivalent but that is fine as it simply goes unused.

transforms.sourceVariable: UKBB:Chest pain or discomfort walking normally
transforms.targetCdeId: Any chest discomfort ever/since last exam or medical history update?
transforms.rationale: Direct Yes/No mapping is straightforward. '-1' (Unable to walk on the level) is a functional limitation response with no equivalent in the chest discomfort CDE. '-3' (Prefer not to answer) is a refusal sentinel with no direct CDE equivalent (CDE has no refusal code; '9=Don't know' is epistemically different from a deliberate refusal).

transforms.sourceVariable: UKBB:Chest pain or discomfort when walking uphill or hurrying
transforms.targetCdeId: Any chest discomfort ever/since last exam or medical history update?
transforms.rationale: Direct yes/no mapping is straightforward. '-1=Unable to walk up hills or to hurry' is a functional limitation response that does not directly indicate chest discomfort (it could be a skip pattern or alternative symptom descriptor with no CDE equivalent). '-3=Prefer not to answer' is a refusal sentinel with no CDE equivalent (CDE has no refusal code; '9=Don't know' is epistemically different from a deliberate refusal).

transforms.sourceVariable: UKBB:Able to walk or cycle unaided for 10 minutes
transforms.targetCdeId: Are you able to exercise for an hour?
transforms.rationale: Source is a simple Yes/No/Unsure question while the CDE captures a 5-level difficulty scale. 'Yes' (1) maps to 'Without any difficulty' (5) and 'No' (0) maps to 'Unable to do' (1) as the closest semantic equivalents, though these are imperfect matches since 'Yes' could include doing it with difficulty. 'Unsure' (2) has no meaningful CDE equivalent and is left unmapped. Confidence is moderate due to the structural mismatch between binary and ordinal scales.

records.concept: Leg pain on walking and claudication characteristics (calf pain, onset at rest)

cde.id: Do you suffer from intermittent claudication [PhenX]

records.idealCde: Chest pain or discomfort presence and characteristics: a set of related items assessing (1) whether the individual ever experiences pain or discomfort in the chest (general screen; categorical: Yes / No / Do not know / Prefer not to answer), (2) whether chest pain occurs during physical activity (categorical: Yes / Unsure / No), (3) whether chest pain occurs at rest or outside of physical activity in the past month (categorical: Yes / Unsure / No), (4) whether chest pain is relieved by standing still (categorical: Yes / No / Do not know), and (5) whether the individual is able to walk or cycle unaided for 10 minutes as a functional exercise-capacity safety screen (categorical: Yes / Unsure / No). Together these items characterize the presence, exertional vs. rest pattern, and relief characteristics of chest pain/discomfort in the self-reporting participant, supporting identification of anginal symptom patterns and pre-exercise safety screening. All items are self-reported by the participant; answer type is categorical single-select per item.

records.concept: Average daily cigarette count (numeric)
records.idealCde: Substance Use — Tobacco and Recreational Drug Use Patterns: A multi-domain CDE capturing self-reported use of tobacco and recreational/non-prescribed drugs. (1) Tobacco — Average daily cigarette count: numeric free-text entry representing the average number of cigarettes smoked per day (integer, units: cigarettes/day). (2) Tobacco — Lifetime cigarette use (≥100 cigarettes ever): binary yes/no radio (Yes / No / Don't know / Prefer not to answer). (3) Tobacco — Quit attempt: among ever-smokers, whether the respondent has ever made a serious attempt to quit smoking (Yes / No / Don't know / Prefer not to answer). (4) Recreational drug use — Sedative use frequency in the past 3 months: ordinal radio capturing self-reported frequency of non-prescribed sedative use (Never / Once or twice / Monthly / Weekly / Daily or almost daily / Prefer not to answer). (5) Recreational drug use — Inhalant use frequency in the past 3 months: ordinal radio capturing self-reported frequency of inhalant use (Never / Once or twice / Monthly / Weekly / Daily or almost daily / Prefer not to answer). All items are self-reported by the participant; no laterality, body-site, or address qualifier applies. Sedative and inhalant items carry an explicit 3-month look-back window.

All items are self-reported by the participant; no laterality, body-site, or address qualifier applies. Sedative and inhalant items carry an explicit 3-month look-back window.

records.concept: Current cigar smoking frequency (every day / some days / not at all)
records.idealCde: Substance Use — Tobacco and Recreational Drug Use Patterns: A multi-domain CDE capturing self-reported use of tobacco and recreational/non-prescribed drugs. (1) Tobacco — Average daily cigarette count: numeric free-text entry representing the average number of cigarettes smoked per day (integer, units: cigarettes/day). (2) Tobacco — Lifetime cigarette use (≥100 cigarettes ever): binary yes/no radio (Yes / No / Don't know / Prefer not to answer). (3) Tobacco — Quit attempt: among ever-smokers, whether the respondent has ever made a serious attempt to quit smoking (Yes / No / Don't know / Prefer not to answer). (4) Recreational drug use — Sedative use frequency in the past 3 months: ordinal radio capturing self-reported frequency of non-prescribed sedative use (Never / Once or twice / Monthly / Weekly / Daily or almost daily / Prefer not to answer). (5) Recreational drug use — Inhalant use frequency in the past 3 months: ordinal radio capturing self-reported frequency of inhalant use (Never / Once or twice / Monthly / Weekly / Daily or almost daily / Prefer not to answer). All items are self-reported by the participant; no laterality, body-site, or address qualifier applies. Sedative and inhalant items carry an explicit 3-month look-back window.

records.concept: Self-reported ABO blood group/blood type

cde.id: Blood Group

records.idealCde: Blood group (ABO type) — self-reported: 'If you know it, what is your blood group/blood type?' Categorical (nominal) response capturing the participant's self-reported ABO blood group. Answer type: single-select categorical; permissible values: A, B, AB, O, Don't know, Prefer not to answer.

transforms.targetCdeId: Blood Group
transforms.rationale: The source variable captures ABO blood group type (A, B, AB, O) without Rh factor (positive/negative), so none of the source codes can be confidently mapped to any specific CDE value (e.g., A+ vs A-). The CDE requires full ABO+Rh typing. All source codes are unmapped: the ABO-only options lack Rh information, and 'I don't know' / 'Prefer not to answer' have no CDE equivalents.

records.concept: Reason contributed to decision to retire (binary yes/no per reason)
records.idealCde: Retirement reason — health/disability contribution: A binary or categorical data element capturing whether health, stress, or disability (physical and/or emotional/mental) was a contributing reason for the respondent's decision to retire (or return to work after retirement). The primary question is: 'Did health, stress, or disability contribute to your decision to retire (or return to work after retirement)?' with a follow-up distinguishing the type: physical health, emotional/mental health (including stress), or both. Answer type: categorical — Yes/No for contribution (binary), and if yes, a three-category response: 1=Physical health only, 2=Emotional/mental health (including stress) only, 3=Both physical and emotional/mental health. Qualifier: self-reported; pertains to the respondent's own retirement decision (initial retirement and/or return to work after first retirement).

records.concept: Employer type when returning to work after retirement (same employer / different employer / self-employed)
records.idealCde: Retirement reason — health/disability contribution: A binary or categorical data element capturing whether health, stress, or disability (physical and/or emotional/mental) was a contributing reason for the respondent's decision to retire (or return to work after retirement). The primary question is: 'Did health, stress, or disability contribute to your decision to retire (or return to work after retirement)?' with a follow-up distinguishing the type: physical health, emotional/mental health (including stress), or both. Answer type: categorical — Yes/No for contribution (binary), and if yes, a three-category response: 1=Physical health only, 2=Emotional/mental health (including stress) only, 3=Both physical and emotional/mental health. Qualifier: self-reported; pertains to the respondent's own retirement decision (initial retirement and/or return to work after first retirement).

records.concept: Current STI status (Yes/No)
records.idealCde: Sexually Transmitted Infections (STIs) status and treatment history: A set of three related data elements capturing self-reported STI history — (1) Current STI status: Does the participant currently have a sexually transmitted infection? [Boolean radio: Yes / No]; (2) Age of onset/diagnosis: At what life stage were they first diagnosed with an STI? [Ordinal radio: Child (0–11) / Adolescent (12–17) / Adult (18–64) / Older Adult (65–74) / Elderly (75+)]; (3) Prescription medication use: Has the participant been prescribed medication for an STI? [Boolean radio: Yes / No]. All three elements are conditioned on a confirmed STI diagnosis (InfectiousDiseaseCondition = SexuallyTransmittedInfections). No laterality, body-site, or address qualifier is specified.

records.concept: Mental health condition current status, life-stage of onset, and prescription medication use (anxiety, depression, other mental health/substance use)
records.idealCde: Mental health condition status and related attributes (current status, age of onset life stage, and prescription medication use) for specific diagnosed mental health conditions (anxiety, depression). For each condition, captures: (1) whether the participant currently has the condition [radio: Yes / No]; (2) the life stage at which the condition was first diagnosed or onset [radio: Child (0–11) / Adolescent (12–17) / Adult (18–64) / Older adult (65–74) / Elderly (75+)]; and (3) whether the participant is currently taking prescription medications for the condition [radio: Yes / No]. Administered conditionally per condition (anxiety, depression) when that condition is endorsed. Subject qualifier: self-report by the participant. Answer type: categorical (radio) for each attribute; no numeric units.
