records.concept: Emotional/psychological distress symptom frequency in the past 7 days (ordinal scale)

cde.id: In the past 7 days How often have you been bothered by emotional problems such as feeling anxious, depressed or irritable?

records.idealCde: Emotional/psychological distress frequency in the past 7 days: captures how often a respondent has been bothered by emotional or mood-related symptoms (e.g., anxiety, depression, irritability, fearfulness, feeling that everything is an effort, or being bothered by things that usually do not bother them) within a 7-day recall window. Question asked: 'In the past 7 days, how often have you been bothered by [emotional/mood symptom]?' Answer type: ordinal frequency scale reflecting days or subjective frequency within the past week; response options range from never/rarely (less than 1 day or never) through some of the time (1–2 days), occasionally/sometimes (3–4 days), to all of the time/always (5–7 days). Note: member fields represent distinct symptom-specific items (general emotional problems, being bothered by things, effort, depressed mood, fearful/tearful) that share the same 7-day frequency response format and together constitute a multi-item emotional distress symptom frequency battery; the ideal CDE captures the shared measurement construct (past-7-day symptom frequency) and ordinal response structure common to all items.

transforms.targetCdeId: In the past 7 days How often have you been bothered by emotional problems such as feeling anxious, depressed or irritable?
transforms.rationale: All five source codes map directly and unambiguously to the corresponding CDE frequency levels (Never=1, Rarely=2, Sometimes=3, Often=4, Always=5). The source variable name also confirms the same 7-day recall window and emotional problem domain as the target CDE.

transforms.targetCdeId: In the past 7 days How often have you been bothered by emotional problems such as feeling anxious, depressed or irritable?
transforms.rationale: The source scale runs from 'All of the time' (most frequent) to 'Rarely or never' (least frequent), while the CDE runs from 'Never' (1) to 'Always' (5). Mapping is reversed and approximate: source 1 (5-7 days, all the time) -> CDE 5 (Always); source 2 (3-4 days, occasionally) -> CDE 4 (Often); source 3 (1-2 days, some of the time) -> CDE 3 (Sometimes); source 4 (less than 1 day, rarely or never) -> CDE 1 (Never). CDE code 2 (Rarely) has no direct source equivalent since source 4 collapses 'rarely' and 'never' together. Confidence is moderate because the frequency bands do not align perfectly and the CDE 'Rarely' category is unrepresented.

records.rationale: Candidate 1 exactly matches the primary member field — same question ('bothered by emotional problems such as feeling anxious, depressed or irritable'), same 7-day window, and same Never/Rarely/Sometimes/Often/Always ordinal scale.

records.concept: Duration in weeks of longest period of feeling depressed or down
records.idealCde: Emotional/psychological distress frequency in the past 7 days: captures how often a respondent has been bothered by emotional or mood-related symptoms (e.g., anxiety, depression, irritability, fearfulness, feeling that everything is an effort, or being bothered by things that usually do not bother them) within a 7-day recall window. Question asked: 'In the past 7 days, how often have you been bothered by [emotional/mood symptom]?' Answer type: ordinal frequency scale reflecting days or subjective frequency within the past week; response options range from never/rarely (less than 1 day or never) through some of the time (1–2 days), occasionally/sometimes (3–4 days), to all of the time/always (5–7 days). Note: member fields represent distinct symptom-specific items (general emotional problems, being bothered by things, effort, depressed mood, fearful/tearful) that share the same 7-day frequency response format and together constitute a multi-item emotional distress symptom frequency battery; the ideal CDE captures the shared measurement construct (past-7-day symptom frequency) and ordinal response structure common to all items.

records.concept: Neighborhood predominant housing type

cde.id: Neighborhood housing main type PhenX

records.idealCde: Neighborhood predominant housing type: categorical variable capturing the main type of residential housing structures in the respondent's neighborhood. Question: 'What is the main type of housing in your neighborhood?' Answer type: single-select categorical with options including: (1) Detached single-family housing; (2) Townhouses, row houses, apartments, or condos of 2–3 stories; (3) Mix of single-family residences and townhouses, row houses, apartments, or condos; (4) Apartments or condos of 4 or more stories; and potentially additional categories (e.g., mobile homes, other). No units; nominal scale.

transforms.targetCdeId: Neighborhood housing main type PhenX
transforms.rationale: All source codes map directly and unambiguously to CDE values. Labels are essentially identical across all six options, including the don't know sentinel.

records.concept: Employment gap activity type: primary activity during periods not in paid employment ≥20 hrs/week
records.idealCde: Employment gap activity type: Categorical variable capturing the primary activity engaged in during periods when the participant was not working in paid employment for at least 20 hours per week ('gaps'). Answers the question: 'What were you doing during this gap period when you were not in paid employment of at least 20 hours per week?' Expected answer type: single-select categorical with mutually exclusive coded categories including at minimum: paid work less than 15 hours/week or lasting less than 6 months; unpaid or voluntary work; full-time or part-time education; looking after home and/or family; unemployed/seeking work; and potentially other activity types (e.g., illness/disability, retirement, travel). One code selected per gap episode per participant.

records.concept: Occurrence of getting to other rooms of home besides sleep room in past four weeks (Yes/No)

records.concept: Frequency of access to culturally/demographically concordant healthcare providers
records.idealCde: A heterogeneous cluster of fields that do not measure a single unified concept: (1) Frequency of access to culturally/demographically concordant healthcare providers — asks 'How often were you able to see doctors or health care providers who were similar to you?' with an ordinal response scale (Always / Most of the time / Some of the time / None of the time / Don't know); (2) Regular use of mineral and other dietary supplements (e.g., fish oil/cod liver oil, iron, zinc, calcium, selenium, multivitamins) — asks which supplements are regularly taken, with a categorical multi-select response; (3) Regular use of other dietary supplements (pilot) — asks which additional supplements (iron, zinc, calcium, selenium, multivitamins, etc.) are regularly taken, categorical multi-select; (4) Regular use of other prescription medications beyond those already listed — asks 'Do you regularly take any other prescription medications?' with a categorical single-select response (Yes / No / Do not know / Prefer not to answer). These fields span at least three distinct domains (healthcare provider concordance experience, dietary supplement use, prescription medication use) and cannot be collapsed into a single ideal CDE. If a single CDE must be chosen, none adequately represents all members; each should be represented as its own CDE: (A) Provider cultural/demographic concordance frequency — ordinal frequency; (B) Dietary mineral/supplement use — categorical multi-select, supplement type; (C) Other prescription medication use — binary/categorical single-select (Yes/No/Unknown/Prefer not to answer).

cde.id: Difficulty remember birth dates AQ

records.idealCde: Self-reported difficulty remembering people's dates of birth, as an item from the Autism Spectrum Quotient (AQ) Social Interactions and Focus subscale. Question: 'I am not very good at remembering people's date of birth.' Answer type: Categorical single-select ordinal scale — 1=Definitely agree, 2=Slightly agree, 3=Slightly disagree, 4=Definitely disagree, with an option for -3=Prefer not to answer. records.concept: Binary yes/no self-reported health, behavior, or medical history question
records.idealCde: Binary yes/no response to a self-reported health, behavior, or medical history question. Captures whether a participant affirms or denies a specific condition, exposure, procedure, or behavior (e.g., recent caffeine consumption before a clinical test, history of a medical screening procedure, use of a medication or contraceptive, occurrence of a condition limited to a specific context such as pregnancy, or clinical management details such as timing of insulin initiation after diabetes diagnosis). The question posed is a closed-ended yes/no inquiry about a single, well-defined event or state. Answer type: categorical single-select; values: Yes / No / Do not know / Prefer not to answer. Note: member fields carry distinct qualifiers spanning multiple different domains (pre-test behavioral exposure, cancer screening history, contraceptive use history, gestational-only disease qualifier, and diabetes treatment timeline), and no single unified qualifier applies across all members.

records.concept: Family history of alcohol use disorder/alcohol-related mental health condition by biological relative type (multi-select: Mother, Father, Sibling, Daughter, Son, Grandparent)

cde.id: Family history of Drug - alcohol problem [PhenX]

records.idealCde: Family history of alcohol use disorder or alcohol-related mental health condition: a checkbox item asking which biological relatives (mother, father, sibling, daughter, son, grandparent, and potentially other specified relatives) have been diagnosed with an alcohol use condition. Answer type: multi-select categorical (one checkbox per relative type: Mother, Father, Sibling, Daughter, Son, Grandparent, [additional relatives as specified]); presence of a check indicates that relative has a diagnosed alcohol use / alcohol-related mental condition. Subject qualifier: biological family members of the participant (not the participant themselves); each option is qualified by the specific relative relationship.

transforms.targetCdeId: Family history of Drug - alcohol problem [PhenX]
transforms.rationale: All source codes indicate a specific family member has an alcohol use condition, which maps to 'Yes' for family history of drug/alcohol problem. The CDE has no 'No' mapping here because the source codes only capture positive instances (presence of alcohol use in a family member). The 'No' CDE value would correspond to the absence of any of these codes being selected, which is a structural/logic-level mapping rather than a code-level mapping. Confidence is moderate because the source codes are indicator flags for specific relatives rather than a single yes/no family history question, requiring aggregation logic to fully harmonize.

records.concept: Description of exercise protocol recommended/administered (free text)

cde.id: Post-exertional malaise researcher determine stimulus protocol description text

records.idealCde: Exercise Test Protocol and Results File Reference: A paired data element capturing (1) a free-text description of the exercise protocol recommended or administered (e.g., ramp rate, target workload, duration, modality such as cycle ergometer) and (2) the filename or file path of the associated results file (e.g., XML file containing raw exercise test readings including ECG data) generated at the conclusion of the test. Answers the questions: 'What exercise protocol was prescribed/used?' and 'What is the name/reference of the file storing the exercise test results and ECG data for this session?' Answer type: Text (free-text description for protocol; alphanumeric filename/path string for results file reference); no units.

records.rationale: Candidate 17 is a free-text description of a researcher-determined stimulus/exercise protocol, which is the closest building block to the concept of a free-text description of an exercise protocol recommended/administered, but needs to be generalized beyond the post-exertional malaise context.

records.concept: Filename/file path of exercise test results file (XML containing ECG/fitness data)
records.idealCde: Exercise Test Protocol and Results File Reference: A paired data element capturing (1) a free-text description of the exercise protocol recommended or administered (e.g., ramp rate, target workload, duration, modality such as cycle ergometer) and (2) the filename or file path of the associated results file (e.g., XML file containing raw exercise test readings including ECG data) generated at the conclusion of the test. Answers the questions: 'What exercise protocol was prescribed/used?' and 'What is the name/reference of the file storing the exercise test results and ECG data for this session?' Answer type: Text (free-text description for protocol; alphanumeric filename/path string for results file reference); no units.
members: UKBB:Fitness test results, including ECG data

records.concept: Household income source receipt indicator (past 12 months): investment/interest/trust income (in trm)
records.idealCde: Household income source receipt indicator (past 12 months): A binary yes/no flag capturing whether the household received any income from a specific source during the past 12 months, asked in the context of total income across all household members. The member fields suggest at least two distinct source categories are tracked — one labeled 'in trm' (likely investment/interest/trust or similar) and one labeled 'se trm' (likely self-employment). Each source is a separate binary CDE. Question: 'Did your household receive any income from [specified source] in the past 12 months?' Answer type: Boolean/binary; coded 1=Yes, 0=No. Note: The source concept spans multiple income-source-specific indicators (e.g., investment income and self-employment income), each carrying a different source qualifier; these should be represented as distinct CDEs differentiated by the income source type rather than collapsed into a single generic field.
